card.intuition: To classify a new point, find its k nearest training points by distance (Euclidean, cosine, etc.) and take a majority vote. KNN is a non-parametric, lazy learner — no model is trained; the entire training set is the model.
card.analogy: Asking your k closest neighbours for a recommendation: if 4 out of 5 nearest neighbours prefer pizza over sushi, predict pizza. The quality of the prediction depends entirely on choosing the right k and having relevant neighbours.
card.time_complexity: O(nd) per query (brute force); O(log n · d) with KD-tree for low d
card.space_complexity: O(nd) to store all training data
when_to_use: Low-dimensional data where geometric distance is meaningful
Quick baseline, anomaly detection, recommendation systems
When the decision boundary is highly non-linear and non-parametric
gotchas: Scale features before KNN — features with large range dominate the distance calculation
k too small → noisy, overfits; k too large → smooth, underfits. Use cross-validation to select k
Curse of dimensionality: in high dimensions, all points are approximately equidistant — KNN breaks down

hints: Take the k nearest indices: distances[:k] — these are the indices sorted by ascending distance
exercise.test_code:
X_tr = [[1,2],[1.5,1.8],[2,1],[5,5],[6,5],[5,6]]
y_tr = ['A','A','A','B','B','B']
knn  = KNN().fit(X_tr, y_tr)
assert knn.predict([[1,1]],     k=3) == ['A']
assert knn.predict([[5.5,5.5]], k=3) == ['B']
assert knn.predict(X_tr,        k=3) == y_tr
print('PASS')
